title: Family Ties

jokes.text: Wow! Look at the beak on that bird.
jokes.setup: Michael has just discovered a photo of a woman who might be his secret sister.
jokes.punchline: His first reaction is to comment on her large nose — framed as if noticing a literal bird, but referring to the woman's prominent facial feature.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: it sounds like Michael is describing the photo composition ('look at the bird'), but he's actually cruelly commenting on the woman's nose. The economy is superb — maximum information in minimum words. The rewatch bonus is significant because later we meet Nellie and the nose comment pays off visually.

jokes.text: Yeah, but that was retard... misguided.
jokes.setup: Michael references his earlier paranoia about 'Mr. F' and tries to describe it as foolish.
jokes.punchline: He starts to say 'retarded,' catches himself mid-word, and substitutes 'misguided' — a self-correcting near-slur that's funnier for the hesitation.
jokes.explanation: The mid-word correction is perfectly timed and character-specific — Michael is the 'responsible' Bluth who polices language, so catching himself is doubly funny. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing the full Mr. F arc makes this even more layered.

jokes.text: Oh, there's the woman I'm sexually attracted to.
jokes.setup: Michael has just declared he can't stand to hear another lie from the family. Tobias appears.
jokes.punchline: Tobias announces his attraction to Lindsay with completely clinical detachment — as if reading from a checklist, not expressing genuine desire. The word 'sexually' does enormous work, signaling the absence of real chemistry.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. Only Tobias would describe his wife as 'the woman I'm sexually attracted to' — it sounds like a clinical identification, not a declaration of love. The word 'sexually' is doing everything: it's redundant, clinical, and reveals the total dysfunction of their marriage in five words. Strong candidate for 8+ because of exceptional character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Lindsay and I are planning a night of heterosexual intercourse.
jokes.setup: Tobias says he's heading to the gym to 'prepare for our tryst.'
jokes.punchline: 'Heterosexual intercourse' — the word 'heterosexual' is the entire joke, implying this needs to be specified and is not the default for Tobias.
jokes.explanation: The specification of 'heterosexual' is perhaps the most Tobias line in the episode. It's a running gag payoff (Tobias's closeted homosexuality) delivered with his trademark oblivious earnestness. You could not put this line in any other character's mouth. Exceeds the 8.0 anchor in character integration.

jokes.text: You can just say intercourse.
jokes.setup: Tobias says 'heterosexual intercourse.'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's weary correction addresses only the redundancy of 'intercourse,' not the elephant in the room ('heterosexual'), suggesting she's too exhausted to even address the real problem.
jokes.explanation: A great straight-woman line. Lindsay corrects the wrong word, which itself is a joke about her being too worn down to address the real issue. The economy is excellent — one short sentence does a lot of character work.

jokes.text: The extended sex-postponement negotiation: seven or eight days → couple of weeks → three weeks → a month → start of next month → 31-day month, even though this particular month has 30 days.
jokes.setup: Tobias and Lindsay agree they need to work on themselves before attempting intimacy.
jokes.punchline: What starts as 'seven or eight days' escalates through increasingly implausible delays to 'a 31-day month, even though this particular month has 30 days' — the narrator's final observation is the perfect punctuation.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured escalation sequence. Each beat logically follows but grows more absurd, building to the narrator's deadpan observation about the calendar — which is a meta-comedy move (the show's narrator calling out the joke's internal illogic). The structure is near-perfect: setup, escalation, escalation, escalation, and then the narrator lands the kill shot. This earns its 8+ score.

jokes.text: Oh, you know what? I've already prepared a list of 'won'ts.' / Oh, you're not going to believe this. My list of 'can'ts.'
jokes.setup: Having agreed to eventually have sex, the couple pivot to conditions.
jokes.punchline: Both have independently prepared lists of prohibited acts — and their matching preparation reveals this 'spontaneous' conversation is actually a well-rehearsed negotiation. The 'can'ts' vs 'won'ts' distinction is a subtle joke about agency vs. ability.
jokes.explanation: The simultaneous revelation of lists is a strong structural beat. 'Won'ts' vs 'can'ts' — one suggests unwillingness, the other inability — is a genuinely clever word choice that implies physical limitations. Well-earned humor from character work built over multiple seasons.

jokes.text: Marital love can be so romantic. / What's that supposed to mean? / What? / Marital love. You were looking right at me, and you said 'marital love.'
jokes.setup: The narrator sarcastically comments 'Marital love can be so romantic' after watching the won'ts/can'ts exchange. Lindsay snaps at Tobias thinking he said it.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's accusatory reaction reveals she finds sarcasm offensive when it comes from her husband — the miscommunication underscores their total breakdown of communication.
jokes.explanation: A clever structural joke — the narrator's commentary bleeds into the scene's reality. This is meta-comedy specific to Arrested Development's narrator device. Not the sharpest execution but a nice layered moment.

jokes.text: We're married? / ...was, in fact, as real and binding as the rice pudding the disoriented patients had thrown at them.
jokes.setup: George Michael discovers his mock wedding to Maeby was legally real.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's description that it was 'as real and binding as the rice pudding the disoriented patients had thrown at them' — referencing the insane circumstances of the wedding.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'rice pudding the disoriented patients had thrown at them' is genuinely inventive and absurd — it reframes a callback with unexpected specificity. The narrator voice landing this detail is quintessential AD.

jokes.text: Oh. Oh, of course. They're married, too. / Each other.
jokes.setup: George Michael, rattled about his own marriage to Maeby, hears the word 'married' and misapplies it.
jokes.punchline: 'Each other' — the clarification that his aunt and uncle are married to each other (not separately) is a dry punctuation to George Michael's anxious spiral.
jokes.explanation: A quick, well-timed beat. The two-word punchline 'Each other' is extremely economical. Functional laugh, nothing more.

jokes.text: Oh, please. Pity the fool who gives me a bunch of jibber-jabber about romance.
jokes.setup: Gob enters complaining his religious girlfriend dumped him.
jokes.punchline: He invokes Mr. T's catchphrase ('pity the fool,' 'jibber-jabber') with complete sincerity, positioning himself as the wronged tough guy despite clearly being the fool.
jokes.explanation: The Mr. T reference works because Gob has the bravado without the substance — it's perfectly self-unaware. Not particularly original (cultural reference humor) but well-deployed.

jokes.text: If someone was always asking you to embrace the Holy Trinity, what would you think they meant? / Gob thought it meant a three-way.
jokes.setup: Gob complains his religious girlfriend dumped him over a 'simple misunderstanding' about the Holy Trinity.
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms: 'Gob thought it meant a three-way.'
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect joke. The setup is layered — Gob asks the audience an open question, creating genuine ambiguity about what he misunderstood, then the narrator delivers the punchline flatly. It's character-perfect (only Gob would misread 'Holy Trinity' this way), structurally tight (question → narrator answer), and quotable. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: What? I thought that's what all you guys lived for.
jokes.setup: After the narrator reveals Gob thought Holy Trinity meant a three-way.
jokes.punchline: Gob's defensive justification — 'I thought that's what all you guys lived for' — as if this is universal wisdom, addressed to an unseen 'you guys.'
jokes.explanation: A good follow-up beat. Gob's inability to recognize his error and his sweeping generalization ('all you guys') is classic character comedy. Slightly weaker than the setup because it's an expected escalation.

jokes.text: The... something of my ways. / Wisdom? / It's probably wisdom.
jokes.setup: Gob says God is going to show him a sign of 'the... something of my ways.'
jokes.punchline: He can't remember the word 'wisdom,' Michael supplies it, and Gob tentatively agrees 'It's probably wisdom' — as if wisdom is just one plausible option.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully economical portrait of Gob's intellectual limitations. He cannot recall the word 'wisdom' even when discussing his own moral failings. 'It's probably wisdom' suggests he's considered other options. Strong character integration — could only be Gob.

jokes.text: You mean we're married? / No. It's just a piece of paper. It doesn't mean anything. / We should shred it. / It's creeping me out. / Yeah. Me... / But, if we just put it over the dresser, like we think it's a joke, and we would always know... / It is a joke. / I know. Unless we get it framed.
jokes.setup: George Michael tells Maeby their mock wedding was legally real.
jokes.punchline: Their mutual denial rapidly escalates — shred it → it's creepy → put it over the dresser → it's a joke → unless we get it framed. George Michael's suggestion to frame the marriage certificate is the punchline: the opposite of shredding it.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured cringe-comedy escalation. The logical movement from 'shred it' to 'get it framed' is a perfect reversal that reveals George Michael's conflicted feelings. The structure is immaculate — each beat undercuts the previous one. The incest undercurrent gives it extra edge without being stated explicitly.

jokes.text: I actually meant Lindsay and Nellie. / You call him that, too? / It's so nice to be able to talk like this.
jokes.setup: Michael visits his parents and mentions 'Lindsay and Nellie,' implying a sister.
jokes.punchline: Lucille thinks 'Nellie' refers to a man ('You call him that, too?') — revealing this is an old family nickname for someone male, subverting the sister theory entirely.
jokes.explanation: A nice misdirection that plants a seed for the episode's resolution (Nellie turns out to be a family nickname for cousin Larry). Lucille's 'It's so nice to be able to talk like this' is a great tag — she's completely unaware of the miscommunication. Good structural work.

jokes.text: So, when do we get to see our new, fancy office? / Soon as you get your new, fancy job. / You're all fired.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains George Sr.'s 'Black Friday' mass firings — employees' first task was often to unload computers from a rental truck.
jokes.punchline: The flashback shows an employee eagerly asking about the new fancy office, and George Sr. calmly responding 'Soon as you get your new, fancy job' before the narrator reveals they're immediately fired.
jokes.explanation: The dark comedy of George Sr. responding to 'new fancy office?' with 'new fancy job' (implying: you won't have one) is sharp. The narrator's reveal about the unloading task being the employees' final job adds a layer of cruel irony. Dark and efficient.

jokes.text: No, the prosecution is going to want to have access to the hard drive, so I just want to make sure everything is nice and clean for them.
jokes.setup: Michael asks what George Sr. is doing with an electromagnet near all the computers.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s explanation that he's 'making sure everything is nice and clean for the prosecution' while actively destroying evidence is delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: A solid irony joke. The sincere framing of evidence destruction as helpfulness for the prosecution is George Sr. at his most brazenly corrupt. The deadpan delivery is key — he's not winking at the camera, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Oh, well, excuse me, Judge Reinhold, but I am trying to protect my family.
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Sr. the evidence destruction is illegal and says 'You can't be judge...'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. interrupts with 'excuse me, Judge Reinhold' — a running gag in the show where the Bluths mockingly invoke the actor's name when someone sounds preachy.
jokes.explanation: A reliable running gag payoff. The 'Judge Reinhold' line is one of AD's more quotable recurring bits — it works because it's both a celebrity name and a pun on 'judge,' and the accusation of being 'judge-y' fits Michael perfectly. The rewatch bonus is moderate since it recurs.

jokes.text: That's not a real gold necklace, is it? / Well, it wasn't really your 50th birthday.
jokes.setup: In the midst of the evidence-destruction discussion, Michael notices something suspicious about his father's jewelry.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s admission that the 50th birthday celebration was also fake is a perfectly casual reveal of sustained parental deception.
jokes.explanation: A compact, devastating joke. The casual equivalence George Sr. draws — fake necklace, fake birthday — delivered in a single subordinate clause reveals layers of his manipulative character. 'Well, it wasn't really your 50th birthday' is delivered with no defensiveness, which is the joke. Excellent economy and character work.

jokes.text: It's bad enough you got Buster faking a coma just so he doesn't have to testify. / I've been trying to get him out of that coma. It's costing us a fortune. He's sleeping us dry.
jokes.setup: Michael accuses George Sr. of having Buster fake a coma to avoid testifying.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s complaint that Buster is 'sleeping us dry' — framing his comatose son as a financial burden, with 'sleeping us dry' as a riff on 'bleeding us dry.'
jokes.explanation: 'Sleeping us dry' is a sharp wordplay variant of 'bleeding us dry' that perfectly captures George Sr.'s mercenary view of his own children. The complaint that his comatose son is costing too much is a great dark-comedy beat.

jokes.text: Okay, faker, I'm cutting the cord. / I'll see you at the snack machine.
jokes.setup: George Sr. threatens to 'cut the cord' on Buster's fake coma.
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately responds — proving he's fully conscious — by casually arranging to meet at the snack machine.
jokes.explanation: The joke's perfection lies in Buster's instant, utterly normal response. He doesn't lurch awake dramatically — he just makes snack plans. The mundanity of 'snack machine' is the punchline. Strong structure and great Buster character work.

jokes.text: She found his complete lack of personality and movement enchanting. / I wish you would say something. Of course, if you did, everything would be ruined 'cause you wouldn't be as pure.
jokes.setup: Buster's nurse has fallen for him while he pretends to be in a coma.
jokes.punchline: The nurse's attraction is explicitly based on him saying nothing — and she acknowledges that if he spoke, 'everything would be ruined.' This is both a commentary on bad romantic idealization and a meta-joke about Buster's personality.
jokes.explanation: A smart joke about romantic projection. The nurse loves Buster *because* he can't talk, which is a sharp observation about how we idealize silent partners — and also a burn on Buster specifically. The narrator's clinical framing ('complete lack of personality and movement') elevates it.

jokes.text: Not a Nick or a Nellie? / Just the one who married Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Michael asks his parents about N. Bluth — is it a Nick or a Nellie?
jokes.punchline: George Sr. responds 'just the one who married Lindsay' — meaning Tobias — implying that 'Nellie' was always a nickname for Tobias (or someone else), completely deflating the sister theory.
jokes.explanation: A good misdirection beat that advances the episode's central mystery. The implication that Tobias is 'Nellie' is funny given how it recontextualizes the name's gender ambiguity in the context of Tobias's character.

jokes.text: Oh, it isn't fun to talk like this?
jokes.setup: Michael is frustrated by his father's cryptic and evasive answers.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s breezy rhetorical question — 'Oh, it isn't fun to talk like this?' — delivered as if confused that Michael isn't enjoying being manipulated.
jokes.explanation: A compact George Sr. character moment. His cheerful obtuseness about his own manipulation is consistent with the character and lands as a mild laugh.

jokes.text: Like Tom here. / I'm 30.
jokes.setup: Michael is reassuring employees that old machines are functional, pointing to a decrepit old computer named Tom.
jokes.punchline: Tom — a person, not a computer — clarifies he is 30 years old. Michael has been referring to a human employee as a broken-down machine.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully economical visual-verbal gag. The setup leads us to think Michael is patting a computer; 'I'm 30' is a perfect two-word reveal. The implication that Tom looks so haggard Michael mistook him for aging hardware is brutal and funny. Maximum laugh per word.

jokes.text: Michael sees 'Nellie' file on the old computer and then 'Gob's Program' listed before it.
jokes.setup: Michael is searching through old computer files for evidence of the mysterious N. Bluth.
jokes.punchline: He first encounters a file labeled 'Gob's Program,' and his one-word reaction is 'Idiot.' Then he finds 'Nellie.'
jokes.explanation: The 'Idiot' reaction to 'Gob's Program' is a perfect one-word character beat. Then the immediate pivot to 'Nellie' drives the plot while the previous beat provides the laugh. Efficient and character-specific.

jokes.text: K-Y Pro, huh? / Hey, don't get any moisture on these bab...
jokes.setup: Tobias arrives while Michael is at the old computer. Tobias notices the product Michael's using.
jokes.punchline: Tobias commenting on 'K-Y Pro' (a lubricant brand) with knowing familiarity, then Michael cutting him off mid-sentence ('these bab...') before he completes something inappropriate.
jokes.explanation: A classic Tobias-says-something-inadvertently-suggestive moment, cut off before completion. The interrupted sentence is a well-used AD device for plausible deniability on the innuendo. Functional, character-specific.

jokes.text: Kind of remember these being faster, you know? / Yeah, well, we were probably all so jacked up on amyl and disco music, we didn't notice.
jokes.setup: They're waiting for a 1970s-era printer to slowly print.
jokes.punchline: Tobias explains they didn't notice the slow speed because they 'were all so jacked up on amyl and disco music' — 'amyl' being amyl nitrite (poppers), a drug associated with gay disco culture.
jokes.explanation: Only Tobias could casually reference amyl nitrite and disco as shared nostalgia. This is another oblivious self-outing moment. The specificity of 'amyl' (not just 'drugs') makes it feel authentic and adds subtext about Tobias's past. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I was hoping to have a jaw session with you. / Well, why don't we just say — as long as that means a talk.
jokes.setup: Tobias wants to talk to Michael and uses an unusual expression.
jokes.punchline: Michael's cautious hedge — 'as long as that means a talk' — implies Tobias's phrasing ('jaw session') sounds potentially sexual, and Michael's learned to always clarify with Tobias.
jokes.explanation: Another entry in the running Tobias-says-something-inadvertently-sexual catalog. Michael's pre-emptive clarification is a nice evolution — he now proactively hedges with Tobias, which itself is funny.

jokes.text: I find myself attracted to someone who is not my wife. / What's her name? / Michael. / Can you believe it? / You're married to my sister.
jokes.setup: Tobias confesses an attraction to someone not his wife. Michael assumes it's a woman.
jokes.punchline: The person's name is Michael — and Michael immediately jumps to 'You're married to my sister,' thinking Tobias means him. The misunderstanding compounds: Michael thinks Tobias is confessing attraction to him.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest sequences. The misdirection operates on multiple levels: (1) we expect Tobias to be attracted to a man, so 'Michael' seems to confirm it; (2) Michael immediately assuming he is 'Michael' reveals his own latent paranoia about Tobias; (3) Tobias's confusion about Michael's confusion is perfectly structured. The 'Don't you think I know that?!' payoff is excellent. Near-flawless execution.

jokes.text: Don't you think I know that?! / I-I'm not interested in you that way. / What way? / Pick one.
jokes.setup: Michael clarifies he's not 'Michael' — Tobias is confusingly in love with someone named Michael.
jokes.punchline: 'What way?' / 'Pick one.' — Michael's exasperated 'pick one' implies there are multiple ways he'd find Tobias's interest unwelcome, covering all bases.
jokes.explanation: 'Pick one' is exceptional in its economy. It simultaneously acknowledges gender ambiguity, Michael's discomfort, and the absurdity of the situation in two words. Genuinely excellent writing.

jokes.text: Interesting that your mind went there, though.
jokes.setup: Michael has just clarified he thought Tobias was expressing attraction to him.
jokes.punchline: Tobias turns it back on Michael — 'interesting that your mind went there' — which is psychologically accurate (Michael did leap to that conclusion) while also being completely rich coming from Tobias.
jokes.explanation: A standout line. The joke works because Tobias is technically right — Michael's mind did go there — but hearing this observation from the most obliviously gay character on television is deliciously ironic. The line has great quotability and character specificity.

jokes.text: I'm afraid the Michael I'm talking about is all lady.
jokes.setup: Michael struggles to understand who Tobias is attracted to.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's phrase 'all lady' is an unusual way to describe a woman — suggesting he needs to emphasize her femininity to himself.
jokes.explanation: 'All lady' is a distinctively weird Tobias formulation — the qualifier 'all' implies he has a checklist or finds it notable. Perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I'll tell you, if I wasn't so dripping and disgustingly sweaty right now, I'd hop over this desk and give you such a hug.
jokes.setup: Tobias is emotional and grateful for Michael's advice not to pursue girl Michael.
jokes.punchline: His description of himself as 'dripping and disgustingly sweaty' delivered with such warmth — as if the sweat is the only obstacle to physical affection — is inadvertently sensual and deeply uncomfortable.
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias — his warmth is rendered uncomfortable by physical specificity. 'Dripping and disgustingly sweaty' as a near-romantic obstacle is a great cringe beat.

jokes.text: Hey, coulda, shoulda, woulda. Hey, hey. No, no. Ooh, look, number's up. / Well, it would've been a seven.
jokes.setup: Tobias has just said he'd hug Michael if he weren't sweaty. The printer finally finishes.
jokes.punchline: Tobias says 'it would've been a seven' about the printed page — implying it's a photo of Nellie rated on a scale — as a non-sequitur that deflates the emotional moment.
jokes.explanation: A decent deflation of the emotional moment. The printer finally producing a 'seven' rating for Nellie is a mild visual gag. Functional rather than inspired.

jokes.text: I believe death is wrong in all of its forms. Except lethal injection, but that's just as a deterrent against more death.
jokes.setup: A politician arrives at the hospital to defend Buster's right to life.
jokes.punchline: His absolutist 'death is wrong in all its forms' is immediately contradicted by his carve-out for lethal injection, which he justifies with circular logic: it deters 'more death.'
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire with excellent structure. The immediate self-contradiction ('except lethal injection') followed by the absurd justification ('deterrent against more death') is a tight, well-executed logical fallacy joke. The politician character is a vehicle for the joke rather than a developed character, but the writing is crisp.

jokes.text: Dignity? Easy for you to say — I'm the one who has to loofah his stump.
jokes.setup: The politician talks about protecting Buster's dignity.
jokes.punchline: Lucille counters that dignity is easy to talk about when you're not the one 'loofahing his stump' — a grotesquely specific physical care task that undercuts the high-minded rhetoric.
jokes.explanation: A genuine standout line. 'Loofah his stump' is grotesquely specific and darkly funny — it uses Buster's disability (the seal/hook running gag) for a perfectly timed deflation of political high-mindedness. Only Lucille could deliver this with such matter-of-fact martyrdom. Quotable and deeply character-specific. Exceeds the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: That is unless, of course, it's as a penalty in a three-strike scenario.
jokes.setup: The politician declares 'no one's killing Baby Buster on my watch.'
jokes.punchline: He immediately adds the three-strikes exception — another self-contradiction of his pro-life position, this one more calculated.
jokes.explanation: A callback to the earlier 'death is wrong except lethal injection' joke, adding another exception (three-strikes). The escalation of the politician's self-contradictions works, though less surprising the second time.

jokes.text: No one's called him Baby Buster since high school.
jokes.setup: The politician refers to Buster as 'Baby Buster.'
jokes.punchline: Lucille's correction implies that Buster was still being called 'Baby Buster' in high school — which is both infantilizing and perfectly on-brand for Lucille's relationship with her grown son.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional character joke in miniature. The punchline implies that calling an adult child 'Baby Buster' was considered appropriate until high school — and then presumably they stopped. The economy (one sentence) and character specificity (only Lucille) make this punch above its weight.

jokes.text: She grew into the nose.
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the bar to find Nellie and finally sees her in person.
jokes.punchline: The narrator comments 'she grew into the nose' — a callback to Michael's opening line ('Look at the beak on that bird').
jokes.explanation: A beautifully executed callback. The narrator's line 'she grew into the nose' pays off the episode's opening line with perfect economy — four words that reference the physical detail, deliver a mild insult, and add information. The structure (open with the nose joke, close the loop here) is textbook AD. Earns its high score.

jokes.text: Michael and Nellie bond over an elaborate, boring locker story that goes nowhere — the narrator cuts away to check on other characters.
jokes.setup: Michael and Nellie have immediate chemistry; Michael starts telling a story about his freshman locker assignment.
jokes.punchline: The narrator literally abandons the scene mid-story — 'Let's see what some of the other folks are up to. Nothing there. Or there... Let's get back to Michael' — treating Michael's riveting locker story as too boring even for the show to follow.
jokes.explanation: An inspired meta-comedy moment. The show's own narrator getting so bored with Michael's story that he checks on other characters (finding 'nothing there, or there') and then reluctantly returns is a brilliant structural joke. It simultaneously characterizes Michael as a boring conversationalist and Nellie as an impressive listener, all through format subversion. Highly original.

jokes.text: Nellie was just a great listener. / Most prostitutes are.
jokes.setup: Michael feels a special connection with Nellie because she listened to his boring story.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan explanation — 'most prostitutes are' — retroactively reveals Nellie's profession to the audience while Michael remains oblivious.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The timing is perfect: the narrator lets Michael rhapsodize about Nellie's listening skills and then drops 'most prostitutes are' as a five-word explanation for everything. Structurally clean, devastatingly economical, and the reveal recontextualizes the entire previous scene. High quotability. Clear 8+ material.

jokes.text: How did you get into this line of work? / Well, I was buried in loan debt from business school, so I started whoring myself out. / Not that there weren't clues.
jokes.setup: Michael asks Nellie how she got into her 'line of work,' still thinking she's a business consultant.
jokes.punchline: Nellie says she 'started whoring herself out' after business school debt — a common expression — but the narrator dryly adds 'not that there weren't clues,' breaking the double meaning.
jokes.explanation: Strong sustained-misunderstanding comedy. 'Whoring myself out' as both idiom and literal fact is a classic double-meaning setup, and the narrator's 'not that there weren't clues' functions as a wry editorial. The structure is tight.

jokes.text: You'd be filling, like, three openings.
jokes.setup: Michael explains to Nellie (whom he still thinks is a consultant) that she'd be working for the whole staff.
jokes.punchline: 'Three openings' is an innocent business phrase that reads entirely differently given what Nellie actually does for a living.
jokes.explanation: A solid unintentional double entendre from Michael, consistent with the episode's running misunderstanding. Less elegant than the 'most prostitutes are' line but functional.

jokes.text: Oh. I heard there were a couple of girls like me consulting over at Enron.
jokes.setup: Michael mentions hiring consultants at Enron as a reference point.
jokes.punchline: Nellie confirms that yes, there were prostitutes at Enron — a quick, dark joke implying that corporate corruption and the hiring of prostitutes went hand in hand.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical political/cultural joke. The Enron reference contextualizes corporate corruption with a dark punchline. It works on both levels of the misunderstanding (consultant vs. prostitute) and is topical enough to land hard.

jokes.text: She's absolutely amazing at that stuff, right? / You do do finances and stuff, right? / I do all sorts of scenes.
jokes.setup: Michael is introducing Nellie to his staff as a financial consultant.
jokes.punchline: Nellie's confirmation — 'I do all sorts of scenes' — uses the word 'scenes' (a sex-work term) rather than 'tasks' or 'work,' which the employees presumably interpret one way while the audience understands another.
jokes.explanation: A continuation of the sustained misunderstanding. 'Scenes' is a nice specific word choice that works on both levels.

jokes.text: Don't be afraid to put her in any position that you want, okay?
jokes.setup: Michael encourages his staff to use Nellie however they need.
jokes.punchline: 'Put her in any position' is innocent office language (staffing positions) but reads very differently given Nellie's actual profession.
jokes.explanation: Another solid double entendre in the sustained misunderstanding arc. 'Any position' is a blunt but effective entry in this running joke.

jokes.text: We're gonna have some ground rules first, okay? No (bleep)(bleep)(bleep) or (bleep). And no (bleep) unless you're wearing a (bleep) or you (bleep)(bleep) me. If this winds up on the Web, I will (bleep) you in the (bleep).
jokes.setup: Michael has just told staff not to be afraid to put Nellie in any position. Nellie responds with her professional terms of service.
jokes.punchline: The heavy censorship of Nellie's conditions — clearly explicit sexual limits — is the joke. The bleeping both protects the show and makes the content funnier through imagination. The final threat is the kicker.
jokes.explanation: The bleeping sequence is a well-executed sustained joke. The audience fills in the blanks, and the imagination does the heavy lifting. 'If this winds up on the Web, I will (bleep) you in the (bleep)' is a great tag. The running bleep gag rewards engagement.

jokes.text: Our computers don't even work on the Internet.
jokes.setup: Nellie threatens what she'll do if her work ends up on the Internet.
jokes.punchline: The employee's flat response — 'our computers don't even work on the Internet' — is both an in-universe fact (the computers are ancient) and a perfect deflation of Nellie's threat.
jokes.explanation: A brief, perfectly timed line that connects back to the episode's B-plot about the old computers. The mundane practicality of the response is funnier than any shocked reaction would be. Great economy.

jokes.text: Hey, I thought tonight we could pop in (bleep) in the old DVD player and sit and watch it maybe just the two of us, husband and wife? / I'm just kidding. / The State of California isn't, but... / You know, that's what makes it funny.
jokes.setup: George Michael has framed their marriage certificate. Now he's suggesting they watch something together 'as husband and wife.'
jokes.punchline: He immediately walks it back — 'I'm just kidding' — then adds 'The State of California isn't' (their marriage is real), then 'that's what makes it funny.' His layered qualification is funnier than the original line.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully layered cringe moment. George Michael's three-stage qualification — joke → walk-back → 'The State of California isn't' → 'that's what makes it funny' — perfectly captures his simultaneous denial and fixation. The cousin-marriage theme is maximally uncomfortable while staying within sitcom guardrails. Character-specific and well-structured.

jokes.text: What the hell is that? / I bought you a weddingring... tone.
jokes.setup: Maeby hears something unexpected from George Michael's phone.
jokes.punchline: George Michael has bought her a 'wedding ringtone' — the pause before 'tone' makes clear he's aware of the double meaning and is trying (badly) to play it off as innocent.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable jokes. 'Wedding ring...tone' with the deliberate pause is a perfect written joke that also plays excellently in performance. The pause communicates everything: George Michael's sheepishness, his awareness, his denial. The wordplay is elegant and the structure (pause as punchline) is inventive. Clear 8+ material.

jokes.text: Opposite of serious.
jokes.setup: Maeby says she's worried George Michael is taking the marriage too seriously.
jokes.punchline: George Michael, having just bought a 'wedding ring tone,' insists 'just the opposite' and then we cut to confirmation he's taking it very seriously.
jokes.explanation: A supporting beat to the wedding ringtone joke. The irony of 'opposite of serious' lands because we've just seen the ring tone purchase. Dependent on the surrounding joke for its impact.

jokes.text: Nellie has blown them all away.
jokes.setup: Michael is getting a positive update on how Nellie is working out with the staff.
jokes.punchline: The narrator describes Nellie's success with the phrase 'blown them all away' — which, given Nellie's actual profession, is simultaneously a business idiom and a literal description.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's double entendre is delivered with perfect deadpan, which is consistent with the show's narrator character. The phone call context (Michael being told 'she's blown them away' while misunderstanding that someone forgot to say 'away') adds another layer.

jokes.text: She's a little bit more like me. / It's like we finish each other's... / Sandwiches? / Sentences. / Why would I say...? / Sandwiches? / That time I was going to say sandwiches.
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining his connection with Nellie to Lindsay. He uses the 'finishing each other's sentences' cliché.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay guesses 'sandwiches' instead of 'sentences,' Michael corrects her, then catches himself — 'that time I was going to say sandwiches' — a callback to the earlier Tobias/Lindsay 'sandwiches' exchange.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single best joke. The callback to the earlier 'sandwiches' misunderstanding is perfectly engineered: Michael sets up the cliché ('finish each other's sentences'), Lindsay accidentally says the previous episode's punchline word ('sandwiches'), Michael corrects her — and then walks it back to confirm he actually was going to say sandwiches. It's a triple-layer joke: parody of cliché, callback payoff, and then a subversion of the correction. The structure is immaculate. This is AD at its highest craft level — a joke that rewards knowing the show's patterns while also working as a standalone absurdist beat. Rivals the series' best moments.

jokes.text: No, you'd just go out and you'd just whore it up, Lindsay, you know?
jokes.setup: Lindsay complains Michael didn't give her access to the banking like he gave Nellie.
jokes.punchline: Michael's frank explanation — 'you'd just whore it up' — is both rude and accidentally ironic, since Nellie is literally a prostitute.
jokes.explanation: The irony of Michael saying Lindsay would 'whore it up' while unknowingly trusting an actual prostitute is a nice layered joke. Michael's bluntness with Lindsay is character-specific.

jokes.text: So I guess you haven't told Nellie what you're thinking. / No, I have not. I've been waiting for the right time.
jokes.setup: Michael is strategically waiting to tell Nellie he thinks she might be his sister.
jokes.punchline: The irony that Michael is carefully timing this reveal while Nellie is actively prostituting herself to his employees is left implicit but lands through dramatic irony.
jokes.explanation: A transitional beat that works primarily through dramatic irony. Not a standout joke on its own but contributes to the episode's sustained comedy of errors.

jokes.text: she's got this old boss that's claiming he wants a percentage. / Yo, man, you owe me money. / I ain't no dummy. / It better be soon or you're going to feel my hand up your (bleep).
jokes.setup: Michael mentions that Nellie has an old boss demanding a cut.
jokes.punchline: The reveal is Gob's puppet Franklin — whose threats ('feel my hand up your bleep') are inadvertently literal since Franklin is a hand puppet.
jokes.explanation: Franklin's threat — 'you're going to feel my hand up your (bleep)' — is a brilliant joke because Franklin IS a hand puppet, making the threat literally true as well as menacing. The use of the bleep preserves plausible deniability. Strong AD-specific character joke with excellent layering.

jokes.text: That's funny. I'm going out tonight for drinks, too, with an exciting new man. / Really trying to make that marriage work, aren't you?
jokes.setup: Michael says he's meeting Nellie for drinks; Lindsay reveals she's also going out with 'an exciting new man.'
jokes.punchline: Michael's response — 'really trying to make that marriage work' — is sarcastic about Lindsay's obvious date, which will turn out to be with Tobias.
jokes.explanation: A setup joke for the payoff when Lindsay and Tobias accidentally 'date' each other. Functional foreshadowing comedy, not particularly strong on its own.

jokes.text: Well, excuse me, Judge Reinhold, but I'm feeling a little pressure here. I mean, if you had to have sex with Tobias in four months, you'd be shopping for a way out, too.
jokes.setup: Michael sarcastically says Lindsay isn't trying to make her marriage work.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay invokes 'Judge Reinhold' (the running gag), then delivers her defense: if YOU had to have sex with Tobias in four months — '4 months' being the renegotiated timeline from earlier — you'd be out shopping too.
jokes.explanation: A strong callback that ties together multiple threads: the Judge Reinhold gag, the sex postponement escalation, and Lindsay's character. '4 months' specifically callbacks the absurd timeline established earlier, showing the writers' architectural precision.

jokes.text: I stand corrected. / And disgusted.
jokes.setup: Lindsay explains she'd look for a way out if she had to have sex with Tobias.
jokes.punchline: Michael's two-beat response — 'I stand corrected... and disgusted' — is a quiet, effective character beat.
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'And disgusted' is the joke. Michael concedes the point and adds his emotional response. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: Oh, it's Tobias. Michael, look, this has got to stop. I mean, flattered? Yes. Interested? Not tonight.
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the bar expecting Nellie and encounters someone at the bar.
jokes.punchline: It's Tobias, who seems to think Michael has sought him out romantically — 'flattered? Yes. Interested? Not tonight' — turning the encounter into another installment of Tobias misreading Michael.
jokes.explanation: 'Not tonight' implies other nights are on the table — a classic Tobias oblivious-self-revelation. The 'flattered? Yes.' format is a great deadpan structure.

jokes.text: It was her idea. She wanted to get a drink. I know it's crazy. I mean, I'm married. And the carbs!
jokes.setup: Tobias explains why meeting girl Michael at a bar is problematic.
jokes.punchline: He lists his objections: being married... and then 'the carbs' — as if caloric intake from alcohol is as serious an obstacle as marital fidelity.
jokes.explanation: 'And the carbs!' is a perfectly timed absurdist addition. The escalation from 'I'm married' (serious) to 'the carbs' (trivial) is a great reversal of moral priorities. The exclamation point on 'carbs' implies it's the graver concern. Strong character integration — Tobias's vanity about his physique is well-established.

jokes.text: That's why sometimes life is about making difficult... / Sandwiches. I know.
jokes.setup: Tobias begins a ponderous life-wisdom statement.
jokes.punchline: Michael pre-emptively completes it with 'Sandwiches' — proving the sandwiches gag has become a shared language between them.
jokes.explanation: The second sandwiches callback in one episode — and it's even better than the first, because now Michael is finishing OTHER people's sentences with 'sandwiches,' showing the gag has infected his own thinking. 'I know' after 'sandwiches' — delivered with exhausted resignation — is perfect. The running gag has reached critical mass and this is its peak in the episode. Tied with joke 57 as a series-best moment for this episode.

jokes.text: You read my mind.
jokes.setup: Michael says 'sandwiches' to complete Tobias's sentence.
jokes.punchline: Tobias confirms 'you read my mind' — meaning he genuinely was about to say 'sandwiches,' completing the absurdist loop.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the sandwiches sequence. Tobias confirming he was going to say sandwiches retroactively validates the absurdity and extends the running gag to its logical absurdist endpoint. Three words, massive laugh potential.

jokes.text: Next to that guy? / What guy? / That guy. / No, that's her. / Him? / That's a girl.
jokes.setup: Michael and Tobias try to identify 'girl Michael' at the bar.
jokes.punchline: Tobias keeps identifying what appear to be men; Michael confirms each one is a woman. The running confusion about gender presentation in the bar is a quick, efficient exchange.
jokes.explanation: A quick visual-gag-via-dialogue sequence. The escalating misidentifications work as a fast exchange but don't reach the heights of the surrounding material.

jokes.text: I think the name 'Michael' is making you look for a man.
jokes.setup: Tobias keeps misidentifying people at the bar.
jokes.punchline: Michael's charitable explanation for Tobias's confusion — he's looking for a man because the name is Michael — which is itself a projection (Michael is named Michael).
jokes.explanation: A decent meta-observation. The joke subtly implies Tobias does look for men regardless of the name, but Michael gives him the charitable read.

jokes.text: So nice to meet you, girl Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael introduces Tobias to the female trainer named Michael.
jokes.punchline: Tobias addresses her as 'girl Michael' — the nickname Michael has been using privately — in front of her face, with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: A quick cringe beat. Tobias using 'girl Michael' to her face is socially oblivious in a characteristic way. Functional.

jokes.text: There's no denying that there's a heat between us. / I'd like to deny that. / I know you feel it, too. / This burning desire. / I-I-I don't feel anything. I feel a burning mutual respect.
jokes.setup: Nellie (who thinks Michael is a client) begins making her professional pitch about their 'heat' and 'burning desire.'
jokes.punchline: Michael's counter — 'I feel a burning mutual respect' — substitutes 'desire' with 'respect,' which is simultaneously a literal denial and an unintentional escalation (he really is developing feelings for his suspected sister).
jokes.explanation: 'Burning mutual respect' is a great line — Michael's earnest correction accidentally sounds as intense as the original phrase. The parallel structure (burning desire / burning respect) is nicely constructed.

jokes.text: I've got an outfit that's going to turn that respect into something fun. / Respect is fun.
jokes.setup: Nellie threatens/promises an outfit that will change Michael's feelings.
jokes.punchline: Michael's literal-minded 'Respect is fun' is delivered as a genuine counter-argument, not a joke — which makes it a joke.
jokes.explanation: Three words, big laugh. Michael's earnest correction ('Respect is fun') is delivered as if he genuinely believes this resolves the argument. It's character-perfect — Michael is the show's most clueless-in-a-specific-way character.

jokes.text: You want to be her pimp? / You got to settle up. / Pimp? You mean that she's...? / A lady of the evening. Working girl. / She turns illusions for money... / ...tricks.
jokes.setup: An unknown voice demands payment and threatens Michael. Michael doesn't recognize the voice.
jokes.punchline: The voice describes Nellie as a 'lady of the evening' then begins 'she turns illusions for money' — and Gob corrects to 'tricks.' The substitution of 'illusions' (Gob's word for magic tricks) for the sex-work term 'tricks' is a perfect character joke.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's true standout moments and one of the great jokes in AD's run. 'She turns illusions for money' — Gob literally cannot say 'tricks' because he's spent seasons insisting the word 'tricks' is beneath him. The joke requires seasons of setup and pays off perfectly. The correction from 'illusions' to 'tricks' exposes both the euphemism and Gob's character in one beat. This approaches the series' very best work.

jokes.text: Franklin is Frank? / Mr. F.
jokes.setup: Michael realizes the pimp threatening him is Gob's puppet Franklin.
jokes.punchline: The narrator dryly says 'Mr. F' — a reference to the entire earlier season subplot about the mysterious 'Mr. F,' now revealed to be Franklin the puppet.
jokes.explanation: A multi-season callback payoff delivered in two words. 'Mr. F' — the ominous mysterious figure of earlier episodes — being Gob's controversial sock puppet is an absurdist resolution that only AD could execute. The narrator's flat delivery of 'Mr. F' is exactly right. This earns its score easily — it's a structural and comedic achievement.

jokes.text: Maybe we can give him a family discount. / Family discount is right, Gob. This is the sister that I've been talking about. / Maybe I should have been getting a family rate.
jokes.setup: Michael reveals Nellie may be their sister.
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately pivots to wondering if he should have gotten a 'family rate' for services previously rendered — then realizes what he's implying.
jokes.explanation: Dark and effective. The escalation from Michael's proposal of 'family discount' to Gob's retroactive claim to 'family rate' implies Gob has already used Nellie's services — a dark beat that lands hard. The show walks right up to the incest line without crossing it explicitly.

jokes.text: How do you know her? / Met up with her after a show. / And you did things with her? / He didn't. / I did. / Well, if you consider crying like a girl doing something.
jokes.setup: Michael demands to know what Gob did with Nellie.
jokes.punchline: Gob admits to having 'done things' — but the thing he did was cry. 'If you consider crying like a girl doing something' — Gob's machismo cannot let him admit emotional vulnerability without a gender-based qualifier.
jokes.explanation: A wonderful character reversal. The buildup implies sexual activity; the reveal is emotional vulnerability. 'Crying like a girl' as Gob's self-deprecation is character-perfect — his toxic masculinity can't admit to crying without minimizing it. Strong structure and character work.

jokes.text: I just want my brother to respect me, you know? / I mean, I don't even have a job. / Look, I could use a man's voice on the phone. / And I like your little puppet show. / I ain't so little when I take my pants off, lady.
jokes.setup: Gob explains why he got involved in Nellie's business — he wants his brother's respect and has no job.
jokes.punchline: Franklin (Gob's puppet) responds to Nellie's comment about his 'little puppet show' with a sexual boast about his size — which is both a standard Franklin raunchy joke and inherently absurd because Franklin is a puppet.
jokes.explanation: Franklin's boast is an absurdist beat — he's making a sexual claim that's literally impossible for a puppet. The joke works because Gob is simultaneously Franklin and the person saying this about himself, and the existential confusion is part of the humor.

jokes.text: Honestly I'd take some of the cry out of that.
jokes.setup: Franklin has just made his boast.
jokes.punchline: Michael's quiet suggestion to 'take some of the cry out of that' — referring back to Gob's earlier admission of crying — delivered as a calm production note.
jokes.explanation: Michael as calm editor of Gob's emotional performance is a funny power dynamic. The callback to 'crying' is quick and effective.

jokes.text: But I am not a pimp, Michael. She needed protection. So I make some threats, wear this hat, and I collect my ten percent. / Yeah, that's a pimp.
jokes.setup: Gob objects to being called a pimp and describes his arrangement with Nellie.
jokes.punchline: He describes the exact definition of a pimp — protection, threats, percentage — and Michael flatly confirms: 'Yeah, that's a pimp.'
jokes.explanation: A classic comedy structure: character denies a label while perfectly describing it; straight man confirms the label. Executed with excellent economy. 'Yeah, that's a pimp' is a great three-word flat punchline.

jokes.text: No, I think that's going to be something big.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests finding Nellie in the family might be the 'sign from God' Gob was expecting.
jokes.punchline: Gob dismisses this (his sister being a prostitute and him being her pimp) as insufficient — he's waiting for something bigger from God. His confidence in deserving a grander divine sign is characteristically delusional.
jokes.explanation: A great character beat. Gob deciding that accidentally pimping his own sister to his family's employees is not the divine sign he deserves shows magnificent oblivious grandiosity. The brevity is perfect.

jokes.text: Michael. / Oh, my God, my husband! / Oh, my God, my wife! / Are you two here for each other?
jokes.setup: Lindsay arrives at the bar for her 'exciting new man' date; Tobias is there for girl Michael.
jokes.punchline: They've both independently arranged to meet people at the same bar and have accidentally 'dated' each other — their shock and indignation at finding each other is played for maximum irony.
jokes.explanation: An extremely well-constructed farce payoff. The structure is architecturally perfect — both characters set up their separate dates, both arrive at the same bar, and the mirror reveals ('Oh my God, my husband!' / 'Oh my God, my wife!') are both outraged despite identical behavior. 'Are you two here for each other?' from Nellie is the perfect deadpan observer line.

jokes.text: - How dare you?! - How dare you?! / - And with another woman! - And with another man! / Wait, wait. You didn't plan this together?
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Tobias discover each other on their secret dates.
jokes.punchline: Both are simultaneously outraged at the other ('How dare you!' in unison), then both accuse the other of the same thing ('with another woman/man') — perfect mirroring that exposes their hypocrisy. Nellie's genuine confusion ('you didn't plan this together?') is the deadpan button.
jokes.explanation: The simultaneous 'How dare you!' exchange is a perfectly structured farce beat. The parallel accusations are ironic because they're exactly equal in offense. Nellie's 'you didn't plan this together?' assumes they're so dysfunctional this must be intentional — which is almost true. Strong structure and character integration.

jokes.text: I just assumed you wanted a Holy Trinity.
jokes.setup: Tobias tries to explain why he assumed this was a coordinated plan.
jokes.punchline: He thought finding them both at the bar meant they were planning a Holy Trinity — the same misunderstanding that got Gob's religious girlfriend to dump him.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant callback that unifies the A and B plots. Tobias's assumption of a 'Holy Trinity' callbacks Gob's opening storyline, suggests Tobias has the same misunderstanding as Gob (or that it's a Bluth household term), and reveals Tobias's desires in one line. The callback structure is exquisite — the Holy Trinity joke pays off twice in one episode.

jokes.text: Absolutely not! / We're a happily married couple. / Yes, who happen to be very much in love with each other.
jokes.setup: Confronted, Lindsay and Tobias perform marital happiness for Nellie.
jokes.punchline: Their declarations of being 'happily married' and 'very much in love' are delivered with the same clinical detachment as all their other statements about each other — the protestation itself reveals the lie.
jokes.explanation: A functional irony beat — the protests of love are hollow in context. Not strong on its own but contributes to the scene's momentum.

jokes.text: Well, you're still going to have to pay Frank.
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Tobias are performing marital bliss for Nellie.
jokes.punchline: Nellie cuts through the performance with a practical business reminder — regardless of the emotional drama, someone owes money to Frank (Franklin).
jokes.explanation: A great deadpan punctuation line. The complete indifference to the emotional scene happening around her is perfectly in character for a professional. The callback to 'Frank' (Franklin) is efficient.

jokes.text: I know who N. Bluth is. It is Nellie. And I found her. / That's your sissy cousin Larry. Even then we knew. That's why we called him 'Nellie.' Do you not know that expression, Michael? I thought we were on the same page here.
jokes.setup: Michael triumphantly tells George Sr. he found N. Bluth — Nellie — his secret sister.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. reveals 'Nellie' was always just a nickname for a sissy cousin named Larry. Michael has gone through the entire episode for nothing.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying structural payoff. George Sr.'s 'I thought we were on the same page' is wonderfully gaslit — he acts as if Michael should have understood this all along. Lucille's earlier hint ('You call him that, too?') pays off here. The resolution is classic AD: the mystery resolves into something mundane (a childhood nickname for a gay cousin) while leaving the larger family corruption intact.

jokes.text: She's not my sister. / I let her into the company. / You didn't give her access to the cash, did you? / She said she liked me. / She's a prostitute! That's her job!
jokes.setup: Michael realizes Nellie is just his father's prostitute, not his sister.
jokes.punchline: His explanation that she liked him — 'She said she liked me' — being immediately countered with 'She's a prostitute! That's her job!' is a devastating deflation.
jokes.explanation: The simplicity of 'She said she liked me' / 'She's a prostitute! That's her job!' is very effective. It reduces Michael's emotional investment to the most transactional possible explanation. George Sr.'s exasperation at his son's naivety is perfectly played.

jokes.text: I just want my brother to envy my money, but he's got that hair. / Why can't I have hair and money and him nothing?
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains why he had a prostitute — it wasn't about sex, it was about making his brother Oscar jealous.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s hierarchy of desires: he wants money, he wants his brother to have nothing — but his brother has hair, which apparently is the unfair imbalance that justified hiring a prostitute.
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully specific and dark joke about sibling rivalry. The revelation that George Sr. hired a prostitute not for sex but to make his brother jealous of his money — and that the whole rivalry is ultimately about hair — is hilariously petty. 'He's got that hair' as the crux of George Sr.'s life choices is character-perfect and absurd.

jokes.text: What? The N. Bluth account? That is your account? / Maybe. / But it's only embezzlement if you spend it outside the company.
jokes.setup: Michael discovers that the N. Bluth account — whose money Nellie just withdrew — is actually George Sr.'s own embezzlement fund.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s qualifier: 'it's only embezzlement if you spend it outside the company' — a completely made-up legal distinction delivered as established fact.
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s invented legal principle — 'only embezzlement if you spend it outside the company' — is a great character moment. His confident delivery of this nonsense legal theory is what makes it funny. Consistent with the show's portrait of the Bluth family's self-serving rationalization.

jokes.text: She got to it. / Well, there goes the company. / Okay, now, hold on now. Let's not panic because I-I know someone who can find her. His name is Frank. / That's Gob's puppet.
jokes.setup: The N. Bluth account has been emptied by Nellie. George Sr. says he knows someone named Frank who can track her.
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately recognizes that 'Frank' is Gob's puppet Franklin — George Sr. is relying on a sock puppet to solve the crisis.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback payoff. George Sr.'s confidence in 'Frank' as a solution — and Michael's flat identification of him as a puppet — works beautifully. 'Well, there goes the company' is also a great deadpan line before the pivot.

jokes.text: I'm going to prison.
jokes.setup: George Sr. realizes his assets are gone and his best lead is a puppet.
jokes.punchline: His flat 'I'm going to prison' — delivered as an accounting of the situation rather than an emotional reaction.
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical character beat. George Sr.'s resignation is funnier than any dramatic response would be. Three words, good laugh.

jokes.text: You haven't heard from Oscar lately, have you? / No. / Okay.
jokes.setup: After the puppet revelation, Michael casually asks about Oscar.
jokes.punchline: The exchange — Michael asks about Oscar, George Sr. says no, Michael says 'Okay' and drops it — implies Michael suspects something is happening with Oscar but decides not to pursue it. The casualness of 'Okay' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A subtle plant for a future plot point about Oscar. The Bluth family's casual acceptance of mysterious disappearances is darkly funny. The rewatch bonus comes from understanding what's actually happening with Oscar.

jokes.text: Hey, Ted, what's going on? / Nellie got all of us our checks and these great new computers and have you been...?
jokes.setup: Michael returns to fire everyone — Black Friday — but finds things better than expected.
jokes.punchline: The employees are thriving — paid, with new computers — and Ted almost reveals how exactly Nellie improved morale, stopped short before finishing the sentence.
jokes.explanation: A functional plot-twist joke that sets up the Nellie resolution. The interrupted sentence maintains the episode's double-meaning running gag. Works in context.

jokes.text: What did you do with these guys, exactly? / They mostly just cried.
jokes.setup: Michael is confused about what Nellie actually did at the office.
jokes.punchline: 'They mostly just cried' — the employees cried to Nellie rather than pursuing sexual services, confirming the office has a morale crisis and that Nellie functioned more as a therapist.
jokes.explanation: A wonderful payoff. The office employees doing what Gob did (crying) mirrors the earlier scene and confirms a company-wide emotional crisis. 'They mostly just cried' delivered flatly is perfectly economical. Also callbacks to 'Tom, I'm 30' — these people are at a difficult life stage.

jokes.text: You've got a real morale problem here.
jokes.setup: Michael asks what she did with the guys.
jokes.punchline: Nellie's professional diagnosis — 'you've got a real morale problem here' — delivered as if she's any business consultant, ignoring the absurdity of the context.
jokes.explanation: The pivot to business-consultant voice for this diagnosis is very funny. It's straight out of the AD playbook — the most bizarre situation generates the most mundane professional assessment.

jokes.text: Well, the fact that you didn't steal it might actually be proof that you're not [my sister].
jokes.setup: Nellie has proven trustworthy by returning the money properly. Michael muses she might actually be his sister.
jokes.punchline: Michael's logic — not stealing is proof she's NOT a Bluth — is a devastating assessment of his own family's ethics.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and a great character encapsulation. The irony is rich: Michael uses honesty as the disqualifying factor for family membership, implicitly confirming that all Bluths are thieves. The economy is superb. Could only be said in this show about this family.

jokes.text: I make 300 grand a year. / Marry me.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to recruit Nellie to work legitimately at the company, telling her prostitution isn't a great living.
jokes.punchline: Nellie reveals she makes $300,000 a year. Michael's instant response: 'Marry me.'
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect two-beat joke. The setup (Michael condescending about her 'line of work') is demolished by the $300K reveal, and Michael's instant 'Marry me' — the second he hears the number — perfectly reveals his own mercenary instincts beneath the moral posturing. 'It's weird on so many levels' as the tag acknowledges the incest subtext. The joke earns 8+ for its precision and the speed of the character reversal.

jokes.text: It's weird on so many levels.
jokes.setup: Michael proposes marriage to Nellie, who he earlier thought was his sister and who is a prostitute.
jokes.punchline: Nellie's 'it's weird on so many levels' is a masterpiece of understatement that simultaneously serves as a meta-commentary on everything that's happened in the episode.
jokes.explanation: An extremely economical button. 'So many levels' is the right qualifier — there are at minimum four levels of weirdness (possible incest, prostitution, the marriage proposal itself, and the 'marry me' coming from someone who has a wife). Clean and well-delivered.

jokes.text: I've seen a sign. / And I want to make this work. / A committed relationship that actually means something. / How about you? You want to do just the two of us? [ON NEXT episode preview]
jokes.setup: In the 'On the next...' preview, Gob announces a religious conversion and commitment.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'committed relationship' pitch to what appears to be a woman concludes with 'just the two of us' — still implying the three-way (Holy Trinity) was the default — asking for confirmation they'll be a couple rather than a trio.
jokes.explanation: The 'next episode' tag extends the Holy Trinity running gag, with Gob now confirming to his new girlfriend that they'll limit themselves to a standard two-person relationship. The fact that he has to specify 'just the two of us' suggests his default is otherwise. Reliable character comedy.

jokes.text: Buster in fake coma — doctor works to revive him dramatically / 'Don't you die. Don't you die on me.'
jokes.setup: In the 'On the next...' preview, Buster's fake coma is 'put to its greatest test.'
jokes.punchline: The dramatic 'don't you die on me!' from a doctor — applied to someone who is faking — forces Buster to maintain the performance even as medical staff try to resuscitate him.
jokes.explanation: A good tag for the Buster storyline. The dramatic irony of 'don't you die on me' addressed to a conscious faker is effective. Works as a preview tease rather than a complete joke.

tmdb_overview: In an effort to get to the bottom of things, Michael tracks Nellie down and hires her as a consultant for the Bluth Co. Unfortunately, Michael discovers Nellie's hands-on approach is more than he bargained for.
insight: Wordplay and character comedy sustain 91-point episode with 1.79 jokes-per-minute density.
